California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

May 4, 1994

Honorable Maurice Johannessen
Member, California State Senate
State Capitol, Room 4082
Sacramento, CA  95814

                                        Re:  Your Request for Informal Assistance
                                             Our File No. I-94-138

Dear Senator Johannessen:

     This is in response to your letter requesting assistance
regarding your responsibilities as a member of the California
State Senate under the new conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ applicable to Legislators.

     Since your advice request refers to future decisions for
which the facts are uncertain at this time, we are treating your
request as one for informal assistance.²  Please note, the
Commission's advice is limited to the application of the law to
the specific facts and circumstances surrounding a specific
decision.  Thus, please contact us for further advice should you
have questions regarding specific decisions.

                                    QUESTION

     Under what circumstances will you have a conflict of interest
with respect to your investment interests and ownership of
commercial property which you lease to Pacific Telephone?

                                    CONCLUSION

     Investments, real property, and income are considered
potentially disqualifying economic interests of yours.  Therefore,

_________________________

1    Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000 - 18954.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2    Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento, CA  95804-0807  •  (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-94-138
Page 2


you may not make, participate in making, or attempt to use your
official position to influence a governmental decision if the
decision will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial
effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on you, or on a member of your immediate family, or on any of your
economic interests. However, you will not be subject to
administrative penalties for making, participating in making, or
using your official position to influence a decision affecting
those interests unless the decision is one specified in Section
87102.5.

FACTS

You own common stock in an amount exceeding $10,000 in the
Bank of America, Pacific Gas and Electric, and City Corp. In
addition, you lease commercial property to Pacific Telephone for
approximately $30,000 per month pursuant to a lease agreement
executed in 1982 between you and ATT. Subsequently, the
commercial lease obligations were transferred to Pacific Bell.

You anticipate voting on legislation which may affect these
entities.

ANALYSIS

Conflicts of Interest, Generally

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest.

Section 87103 specifies that an official has a financial
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally, on the official or a
member of his or her immediate family or on certain specified
economic interests.

Thus, all public officials, including members of the
Legislature, will have a conflict of interest in any decision
which will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on their economic interests. (See generally, Isenberg Advice
Letter, No. I-89-343.)

1. Economic Interests

An official has a financial interest in a decision within the
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that a
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable

Our File No. I-94-138
Page 3


from its effect on the public generally,3 on the official or a
member of his or her immediate family or certain specified
economic interests. Section 87103 sets forth economic interests
that are recognized by the Act as follows:

     (a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

     (b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

     (c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

                         Section 87103(a) - (c).

     You have the following economic interests which may be
affected by your legislative decisions:

     1. Investment Interests: You have investments in the form
of common stock worth $1,000 or more in various business entities;
the Bank of America, Pacific Gas and Electric, and City Corp.
Therefore, the common stock you hold in each of these entities
constitutes an investment interest4 as described in Section

___________________________
     3 Regulation 18703 provides an exception to the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Act if the effect of the decision on
the official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally. For the "public generally" excep-
tion to apply, a decision must affect the official's interests in
substantially the same manner as it would affect a significant
segment of the public. These terms have been further refined in
Section 87102.6, further discussed in this letter.

     4 The term "investment" as used in Section 87103(a) above
includes a financial interest in a business entity, including an
ownership interest owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by
the public official, or his or her immediate family, if the
business entity owns real property within the jurisdiction.
(Section 82034.) "Jurisdiction" means the state with respect to a
state agency, including the Legislature. (Section 82035; Section
82049.) Finally, a "business entity" includes any organization or
enterprise operated for profit. (Section 82005.)

Our File No. I-94-138
Page 4


87103(a) where the business entities own any real property in the
state.

2. Real Property Interests: You have an interest in real
property worth $1,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).)

3. Sources of Income: Any person or business that has made
a payment of $250 or more to you in the past 12 months is a source
of income to you for the purposes of Section 87103(c). Therefore,
Pacific Telephone is a source of income to you.5

Accordingly, you may not make, participate in making, or
attempt to use your official position to influence a governmental
decision if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally, on you, or on a member of your immediate family,
or on any of the economic interests specified above.

2. Foreseeability and Materiality

It would appear that it is foreseeable that your decisions to
vote on legislation may have some financial effect on the
businesses in question or your real property interests. Of
course, whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is
made depends on the specific facts of a particular decision.

However, for your economic interests in a decision to be
disqualifying, the financial effect of the decision must also be
material. The standard for materiality differs depending on
whether the economic interest is directly or indirectly involved
in the decision.

A. Direct Involvement

You ask about potential conflicts with respect to the


5 In addition, if ATT is a parent or subsidiary of, or is
otherwise related to, Pacific Telephone, ATT would also be a
source of income to you within the meaning of Section 87103(c).
(Regulation 18706; Regulation 18236.) According to our telephone
conversation of May 2, 1994, ATT is not a parent or subsidiary of,
or is otherwise related to Pacific Telephone.

6 An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not
required. However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is
not reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops.
198.)

Our File No. I-94-138
Page 5


business entities⁷ in which you hold investment interests or which
are sources of income of yours. Generally, if the business is
directly involved in a decision, the effect is deemed to be
material. (Regulation 18702.1.) A business entity is directly
involved in a decision before the Legislature when the business
initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or; is a
named party in, or the subject of, the proceeding. It would
appear that in most cases business entities will be indirectly
involved in the decisions of the California State Senate as a
result of the legislative process.

B. Indirect Involvement

If a business in which you have an investment interest in or
is a source of income to you is not directly involved in the
decision, or the effect of the decision on the business is
involvement analysis is not considered material, you may still be
required to disqualify yourself if the indirect effect of the
decision on the business is material. Indirect effects on a
business interest may be a more common concern since most of the
decisions you will be involved in appear to be rules of general
application.

Regulation 18702.2 provides different thresholds of
materiality for the following:

1. Business entities listed on the New York Stock Exchange
or the American Stock Exchange, or business entities on the
Fortune Magazine Directory of the 500 largest U.S. industrial
corporations or the 500 largest U.S. nonindustrial corporations.
(Regulation 18702.2(a) and (d).)

2. Business entities listed on the National Association of
Securities Dealers National Market List or any business entity
with net tangible assets of at least $18,000,000 and pre-tax
income for the last fiscal year of at least $2,500,000.
(Regulation 18702.2(b) and (e).)

3. Business entities not fitting the requirements of (a) or
(b) but which are listed on the Pacific Stock Exchange or qualify
for public sale in California and are listed on the Eligible
Securities List maintained by the California Department of
Corporations. Or, business entities with net tangible assets of
at least $4,000,000, and had pre-tax income for the last fiscal
year of at least $750,000, with net income from that period of at
least $400,000. (Regulation 18702.2(c) and (f).)

___________________________
7 You have not asked about potential conflicts with respect to a
business entity in which you may be a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or hold any position of management. (Section
87103(d).) To the extent that Section 87103(d) is applicable, the
same analysis would apply.

Our File No. I-94-138
Page 6


4. For any business entity not covered in one of the
categories set forth above, Regulation 18702.2(g) governs.

For example, Regulation 18702.2(a) provides that for a
business entity which is listed on the Fortune Magazine Directory
of the 500 largest U.S. Industrial Corporations, that the effect
of a decision is material if:

(1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease to the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of ... $1,000,000 or more; or

(2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of ... $250,000 or more;
or

(3) The decision will result in the increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of ... $1,000,000 or more.

Thus, if subdivision (a) is the applicable provision, the
effect of a decision which could foreseeably increase or decrease
the gross revenues, assets or liabilities of the business by
$1,000,000 or more, increase or decrease expenses by $250,000
or one of the businesses would be material.

You also have a real property interest in commercial
property. The applicable standard to determine whether a decision
would have a material financial effect on your real property
interests is provided in Regulation 18702.3. You have provided no
facts which would indicate that this regulation is applicable.

3. Is the Decision Specified in Section 87102.5?

The Act provides for civil, criminal, and administrative
penalties for violations of the conflict-of-interest provisions of
the Act. Members of the Legislature, however, are only subject to
administrative penalties, and the penalties may be imposed for
violations of the conflict-of-interest provisions only in a
connection with the decisions specified in Section 87012.5.

Section 87012.5 provides in pertinent part that only the
following types of decisions are affected:

(1) Any state governmental decision, other
than any action or decision before the Legislature,
made in the course of his or her duties as a
member.

Our File No. I-94-138
Page 7


(2)  Approval, modification, or cancellation
of any contract to which either house or a
committee of the Legislature is a party.

(3)  Introduction as lead author of any
legislation that the member knows or has reason to
know is nongeneral legislation.

(4)  Any vote of a legislative committee or
subcommittee on what the member knows or has reason
to know is nongeneral legislation.

(5)  Any roll call vote on the Senate or
Assembly floor on an item which the member knows is
nongeneral legislation.

Please note that subdivisions (a)(3), (4), and (5) of Section
87102.5 apply only to conduct with respect to nongeneral
legislation.  Therefore, although you would have a conflict of
interest in voting on bills that have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on your economic interests, your are
only subject to penalties for such conduct if the bills are
nongeneral legislation as defined in Sections 87102.5 and 87102.6.

Section 87102.6 provides legislation is nongeneral if both of
the following apply:  (1) it must be reasonably foreseeable that
the legislation will have a direct and significant financial
impact on one or more identifiable persons, or one or more
identifiable pieces of real property and (2) it must not be
reasonably foreseeable that the legislation will have a similar
impact on the "public generally" or on a "significant segment" of
the public. (Section 87102.6(a).)

Section 87102.6(b) defines these terms.  Section 87102.6(b)
provides, in pertinent part:

(2)  The term "public generally" includes an
industry, trade, or profession.

(3)  Any recognized subgroup or specialty of
the industry, trade, or profession constitutes a
significant segment of the public.

(4)  A legislative district, county, city, or
special district constitutes a significant segment
of the public.

(5)  More than a small number of persons or
pieces of real property is a significant segment of
[sic] public.

Our File No. I-94-138
Page 8


     (6) Legislation, administrative action, or
other governmental action impacts in a similar
manner all members of the public, or all members of
a significant segment of the public, on which it
has a direct financial effect, whether or not the
financial effect on individual members of the
public or the significant segment of the public is
the same as the impact on the other members of the
public or the significant segment of the public.

Summary

     You may not make, participate in making, or attempt to use
your official position to influence a governmental decision if the
decision will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial
effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on you, or on a member of your immediate family, or on any of your
economic interests.

     It is possible that your decisions to vote on legislation may
have some foreseeable financial effect on the businesses in
question or your real property interests.  However, for your
economic interests in a decision to be disqualifying, the
financial effect of the decision must also be material.  The
standards for materiality which are applicable to you are set
forth in Regulations 18702.1, 18702.2 and 18702.3.

     Nevertheless, please note that Members of the Legislature are
only subject to administrative penalties, and the penalties may be
imposed for violations of the conflict-of-interest provisions only
in connection with the decisions specified in Section 87102.5.
Subdivisions (a)(3), (4), and (5) of Section 87102.5 apply only to
conduct with respect to nongeneral legislation.  Therefore, you
would only be subject to the applicable remedies pursuant to
Section 87102.5 of the Act with respect to those decisions
enumerated in Section 87102.5, and in regards to legislation, only
those decisions concerning nongeneral legislation.

     I trust this letter has addressed your concerns.  Should you
have any further questions regarding this matter or a specific
decision that you would like advice on, please feel free to
contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: Luisa Menchaca
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

Legislation, administrative action,
or
other governmental action impacts in a similar
manner all members of the public, or all members of
a significant segment of the public,
on which it
has a direct financial effect, whether or not the
financial effect on individual members of the
public or the significant segment of the public is
the same as the impact on the other members of the
public or the significant segment of the public.
Summary
You may not make, participate in making,
or attempt to use
your official position to ihfluence a governmental decision if the
decision will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial
effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on you,
or on a member of your immediate family, or on any of your
economic interests.
It is possible that your decisions to vote on legislation may
have some foreseeable financial effect on the businesses in
question or your real property interests.
However,
for your
economic interests in a decision to be disqualifying, the
financial effect of the decision must also be material.
The
standards for materiality which are applicable to you are set
forth in Regulations 18702.1,
18702.2
and 18702.3.
Nevertheless, please note that Members of the Legislature are
only subject to administrative penalties,
and the penalties may be
imposed for violations of the conflict—of—interest prdvisions only
in connection with the decisions specified in Section 87102.5.
Subdivisions
(a) (3),
(4),
and
(5)
of Section 87102.5 apply only to
conduct with respect to nongeneral legislation.
Therefore,
you
wou-ld only be subject to the applicable remedies pursuant to
Section 87102.5 of the Act with respect to those decisfons
enumerated in Section 87102.5,
and in regards to legislation,
only
those decisions concerning nongeneral legislation.
I trust this letter has addressed your concerns.
Should you
have any further questions regarding this matter or a specific
decision that you would like advice on, please feel free to
contact me at
(916)
322—5901.

embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission May 4, 1994 Honorable Maurice Johannessen Member, California State Senate State Capitol, Room 4082 Sacramento, CA 95814 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-94-138 Dear Senator Johannessen: This is in response to your letter requesting assistance regarding your responsibilities as a member of the California State Senate under the new conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ applicable to Legislators. Since your advice request refers to future decisions for which the facts are uncertain at this time, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² Please note, the Commission's advice is limited to the application of the law to the specific facts and circumstances surrounding a specific decision. Thus, please contact us for further advice should you have questions regarding specific decisions. QUESTION Under what circumstances will you have a conflict of interest with respect to your investment interests and ownership of commercial property which you lease to Pacific Telephone? CONCLUSION Investments, real property, and income are considered potentially disqualifying economic interests of yours. Therefore, _________________________ 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections 18000 - 18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 Our File No. I-94-138 Page 2 you may not make, participate in making, or attempt to use your official position to influence a governmental decision if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on you, or on a member of your immediate family, or on any of your economic interests. However, you will not be subject to administrative penalties for making, participating in making, or using your official position to influence a decision affecting those interests unless the decision is one specified in Section 87102.5. FACTS You own common stock in an amount exceeding $10,000 in the Bank of America, Pacific Gas and Electric, and City Corp. In addition, you lease commercial property to Pacific Telephone for approximately $30,000 per month pursuant to a lease agreement executed in 1982 between you and ATT. Subsequently, the commercial lease obligations were transferred to Pacific Bell. You anticipate voting on legislation which may affect these entities. ANALYSIS Conflicts of Interest, Generally Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. Section 87103 specifies that an official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on
